title: Quick check: Jobs, CronJobs, ConfigMaps, Secrets

questions.prompt: What's the most important honest truth about Kubernetes Secrets?

choices.text: Base64 is encoding, not encryption — anyone with cluster read access on the Secret can decode it trivially. Secrets are only encrypted at rest in etcd if you've explicitly enabled etcd encryption-at-rest (which managed services often do by default but self-managed clusters require explicit configuration). The real security model: RBAC controls who can read the Secret in the API; etcd encryption controls whether disk access gives attackers the values. For production-grade secret management, K8s Secrets are best used as the delivery mechanism with an external system as the source of truth: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault holds the real secrets with proper access control, rotation, and audit; External Secrets Operator syncs them into K8s Secrets; pods consume from K8s Secrets as usual. This gives you rotation, audit, and access control that pure K8s Secrets don't provide. The 'put your secret in a YAML file and commit to git' pattern is a 2017-era approach that produces avoidable leaks.

questions.explanation: K8s Secrets are a delivery mechanism with some safety features (RBAC, optional etcd encryption). Use external systems as the source of truth for real secret management.

questions.prompt: A CronJob to send a nightly report has been silently failing for 2 weeks. The team only noticed when stakeholders asked where the reports went. What's the systemic fix?

choices.text: Monitor and alert on Job/CronJob failures. CronJob success isn't automatic visibility — by default, failed Jobs accumulate in the cluster but no human gets notified. Set up monitoring: Prometheus + kube-state-metrics exposes Job status metrics (`kube_job_failed`, `kube_cronjob_status_last_schedule_time`, etc.); alerts when a Job fails or when a CronJob hasn't succeeded recently. For mission-critical scheduled work, also: synthetic monitoring that verifies the expected output exists (an email arrived, a file was written); explicit success signals from the Job itself (write a heartbeat to a tracker); review of `failedJobsHistoryLimit` so failures are visible long enough to investigate; runbooks for the specific Job type. CronJobs are easy to set up and easy to forget — they run silently until they don't, and 'silent failure for weeks' is a routine pattern unless monitoring is in place from day one. Treat scheduled work as production code with the same observability discipline as user-facing services.

questions.explanation: Silent CronJob failure is a routine production pattern. Monitoring + alerts catch it; without them, failure is invisible until the missing output is noticed. Treat scheduled work as production code.
